Title: Role of Anthocyanidin Reductase, Encoded by BANYULS in Plant Flavonoid Biosynthesis

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: DFR-like short-chain dehydrogenase/reductase family; anthocyanidin reductase
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Condensed tannin/proanthocyanidin branch downstream of anthocyanidins
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Downstream, branch step immediately after anthocyanidin formation
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key branch-determining step for CT formation; rate-limiting not established
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms key monomeric building blocks for condensed tannin biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Seed coat/endothelium in Arabidopsis; ectopic accumulation in tobacco petals and Arabidopsis leaves upon transgene expression
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Beneficial for animal and human health; antioxidant activity; contributes to flavor and astringency; improves ruminant nitrogen utilization and reduces pasture bloat
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes condensed tannin accumulation and reduces anthocyanin accumulation; ban mutants lose CTs, while ectopic BAN expression induces CTs in nonaccumulating tissues
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Arabidopsis BAN and Medicago truncatula BAN encode anthocyanidin reductase rather than the previously hypothesized leucoanthocyanidin reductase. 2. Recombinant BAN converts anthocyanidins to 2,3-cis-flavan-3-ols including (-)-epicatechin in NADPH/NADH-dependent assays. 3. Constitutive BAN expression in tobacco petals and Arabidopsis leaves decreases anthocyanins and causes ectopic condensed tannin accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern: AtBAN is expressed in seed endothelium at onset of fertilization through the preglobular embryo stage; ectopic transgene expression detected in Arabidopsis leaves
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Origin_Species: MtBAN is strongly expressed in young developing seeds, weakly in open flowers and flower buds, and very weakly in leaves
Pathway_Genes.Promoters: Cauliflower mosaic virus 35S promoter (constitutive) for transgenic expression
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Recombinant protein expression in Escherichia coli; enzyme assays with anthocyanidin substrates and NADPH/NADH; HPLC; HPLC-MS; GC-MS; circular dichroism; transgenic overexpression in tobacco and Arabidopsis; DMACA staining; butanol-HCl CT quantification; mutant analysis of ban and tt18/ANS backgrounds
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: cDNA library random sequencing; GenBank accessions AY184243 and AF092912
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Ectopic BAN expression reduces anthocyanin pigmentation in tobacco petals; possible flavor/astringency changes implied by altered flavonoid composition
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for engineering condensed tannin accumulation in forage crops such as alfalfa to improve bloat safety, nitrogen utilization, silage quality, and reduce greenhouse gas and urine-N losses; also potentially for modifying health properties, flavor, and astringency in fruits, vegetables, tea, and wine
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: BAN was previously proposed to encode LAR, but this paper provides biochemical evidence that it is ANR. Minor trans products were interpreted as epimerization artifacts. Identical catalytic activity was observed for MtBAN and AtBAN.

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: DFR-like short-chain dehydrogenase/reductase family; anthocyanidin reductase
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Condensed tannin/proanthocyanidin branch downstream of anthocyanidins
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Downstream, branch step immediately after anthocyanidin formation
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key branch-determining step for CT formation; rate-limiting not established
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: Anthocyanidins supplied by endogenous tobacco flavonoid pathway
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: (-)-Epicatechin-derived CTs (inferred from BAN activity and CT staining/quantification)
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms monomeric CT precursors and diverts flux from anthocyanin branch
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Antioxidant and beneficial dietary flavonoids; contribute to flavor and astringency
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Constitutive MtBAN expression in tobacco petals reduces pink anthocyanin pigmentation and induces ectopic CT accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Heterologous expression of MtBAN in tobacco petals causes strong loss of anthocyanin pigmentation. 2. DMACA staining and butanol-HCl assays confirm ectopic CT accumulation in petals. 3. BAN can function in vegetative/floral tissues when flavonoid substrate is available.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern: High ectopic transcript expression in leaves of selected transgenic lines under 35S promoter
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Origin_Species: MtBAN is strongest in young developing seeds of Medicago truncatula
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transgenic overexpression in tobacco; RNA gel blot; petal anthocyanin quantification; DMACA staining; microscopy; butanol-HCl CT quantification
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss of flower pigmentation due to anthocyanin depletion
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Supports use of BAN transgenes to engineer condensed tannins into tissues/crops lacking them
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Study species here is tobacco, but the BAN gene source is Medicago truncatula; this distinction is maintained as requested.
